Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I work part-time to make ends meet since money is tight, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find. I'd usually try to do hands-on practice with the material—like working through problems physically or making diagrams—because that's how things stick for me. I aimed for about 20 hours a week, but it was scattered across odd hours, often late at night after my shifts.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and when I had work scheduled. Some weeks I'd have a decent rhythm going, but then my manager would change my shifts or an assignment would creep up on me, and everything would get thrown off. I tried to keep Sundays for studying, but that didn't always happen. Deadlines were really what drove when I'd buckle down and focus.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if I'd missed something important—that fear of falling behind was always in the back of my mind. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges, which felt kind of silly but also motivating.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where I got into a groove of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before heading out. The gamified elements helped with that—I liked seeing the little achievements pop up. But then things got stressful with work and I fell out of the habit. It never became truly automatic for me, more like something I had to consciously remind myself to do.
Q5: The video lectures were really useful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to work through something hands-on alongside them. I watched a lot of video content—probably more than most people in my class. The discussion boards were also helpful when I was stuck because other students would explain things in different ways. Anything I could interact with actively felt more valuable than just reading.
Q6: I'll admit I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was frustrating. I also skipped some of the optional quizzes when I was overwhelmed. If something felt passive or like it would take forever without clear payoff, I'd push it aside for later—and later often never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something I'd be tested on. The gamified tasks were great for this because I got instant feedback and could see myself progressing. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything where I could do something rather than just read about it felt valuable. If it was just busywork with no clear purpose, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Required activities had this weight to them—like, I knew I had to do them even if I was exhausted or scared about my performance. Optional ones felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise. But weirdly, some optional activities like the gamified challenges were more engaging because there was no pressure of failing. When something's optional, I either really want to do it or I completely ignore it.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had wifi at the library, then study them offline at home where my internet is unreliable. I also made physical flashcards and did a lot of practice problems in notebooks. My roommate and I would quiz each other sometimes, which helped a ton but obviously wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be working through downloaded materials or practicing problems by hand, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd watch a video once online, download it, then rewatch it multiple times offline while taking notes. My actual study time was way more than what my login frequency would suggest. The system only sees when I'm connected, not when I'm actually learning.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my internet situation being what it is, I learned to grab everything I needed in one session and then work with it offline for days. I'd access a module once, download everything available, and then not log back in until I needed something new. It was more efficient for me, but I know it made my engagement look lower than it really was.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming and I got scared about whether I could actually pass. When work picked up extra shifts around midterms, I barely had energy to think about coursework. The stress was intense even though I tried not to let anxiety get to me. I'd stare at the LMS and feel this fear about how behind I was, which made it harder to even start.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—after a long shift, logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the platform because seeing all the tasks I didn't understand made me feel worse. Competing responsibilities were constant; I had to prioritize work because I need the money. The LMS often got whatever energy I had left over.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about bills or work drama or just be too tired to actually absorb anything. I might have technically "accessed" content for an hour but retained maybe ten minutes of it. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than real learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was fear—I was scared of doing them wrong and getting a bad grade. Part of it was just being drained from everything else in my life. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow always brought its own problems. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd drift and procrastinate. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly find the energy to power through, even if I was exhausted. The LMS would probably show spikes of activity right before due dates. It's not ideal, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my fear and get me moving.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback or constructive comments, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. The gamified task feedback was immediate and that kept me coming back. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum. Knowing someone actually looked at my work and responded made the whole thing feel less like shouting into a void.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it could be overwhelming when multiple things were due at once. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized so I could plan my downloads and offline study. The gamified elements added some structure that I actually enjoyed because it broke things into smaller, achievable chunks. Without that, I might have felt even more lost.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they gave me small wins when I needed them and made the course feel less intimidating. What made it harder was when resources required constant internet access or when instructions were unclear. Also, having so many different types of activities made it hard to prioritize with my limited time. Simpler, clearer pathways would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—logging in, downloading stuff, watching videos—but then struggling to actually complete and submit the assignments. Fear of failure can paralyze you even when you're actively trying to learn. Or maybe they're engaging with content but life gets in the way of finishing things. High activity doesn't always mean you have the time or confidence to submit work.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my activity logs. My exam score and assignment scores were decent enough to pass because I was actually studying, just not always in visible ways. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was—just in ways the system couldn't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which is useful for spotting obvious disengagement. But they miss everything that happens offline, the quality of engagement when you are online, and all the emotional and practical barriers that affect participation. They can't tell if someone is scared, exhausted, or working two jobs. Numbers only tell part of the story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just logging in or clicking through videos—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement looks like making flashcards at my kitchen table at midnight or discussing problems with my roommate. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always have external proof.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was sometimes, or how much I was juggling with work and money stress. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the peer support that kept me going. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation and high stress, I kept showing up and trying. My resilience and my actual effort—those are invisible to the data, but they're what got me through.
